steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage has two parts separated by the blank. The first part ("Deposits of crushed volcanic rock… dazzles [blank] visitors") needs to end cleanly. The second part ("When they take a bit of sand home, though, it disturbs the beach's ecosystem…") is a full independent clause with its own subject ('it') and verb ('disturbs') — it can stand alone as a sentence. The blank must produce a punctuation choice that correctly separates these two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'visitors when': No punctuation between the two clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — 'visitors, when': A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — this is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'visitors. When': A period ends the first independent clause and starts a new sentence. "When they take… it disturbs…" is a complete sentence (the 'when' clause modifies 'it disturbs'). Two properly punctuated sentences — correct. ✓
  • Choice D — 'visitors and when': 'And when' would require parallel structure with another main clause, but none follows; this creates an awkward, ungrammatical construction. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
